name: py

metadata.name: py
metadata.summary: library with cross-python path, ini-parsing, io, code, log facilities

documentation:
```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/py.svg
:target: https://pypi.org/project/py
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/conda/vn/conda-forge/py.svg
:target: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/py
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/py.svg
:target: https://pypi.org/project/py
```

```{image} https://github.com/pytest-dev/py/workflows/build/badge.svg
:target: https://github.com/pytest-dev/py/actions
```

**NOTE**: this library is in **maintenance mode** and should not be used in new code.

The py lib is a Python development support library featuring
the following tools and modules:

- `py.path`:  uniform local and svn path objects  -> please use pathlib/pathlib2 instead
- `py.apipkg`:  explicit API control and lazy-importing -> please use the standalone package instead
- `py.iniconfig`:  easy parsing of .ini files -> please use the standalone package instead
- `py.code`: dynamic code generation and introspection (deprecated, moved to `pytest` as a implementation detail).

**NOTE**: prior to the 1.4 release this distribution used to
contain py.test which is now its own package, see <https://docs.pytest.org>

For questions and more information please visit <https://py.readthedocs.io>

Bugs and issues: <https://github.com/pytest-dev/py>

Authors: Holger Krekel and others, 2004-2017

modules.name: py._code._py2traceback
documentation: copied from python-2.7.3's traceback.py
CHANGES:
- some_str is replaced, trying to create unicode strings

functions.name: py._code._py2traceback.format_exception_only
documentation:
Format the exception part of a traceback.

The arguments are the exception type and value such as given by
sys.last_type and sys.last_value. The return value is a list of
strings, each ending in a newline.

Normally, the list contains a single string; however, for
SyntaxError exceptions, it contains several lines that (when
printed) display detailed information about where the syntax
error occurred.

The message indicating which exception occurred is always the last
string in the list.

functions.name: py._code._py2traceback._format_final_exc_line
documentation: Return a list of a single line -- normal case for format_exception_only

functions.name: py._code.assertion._format_explanation
documentation:
This formats an explanation

    Normally all embedded newlines are escaped, however there are
    three exceptions: 
{, 
} and 
~.  The first two are intended
    cover nested explanations, see function and attribute explanations
    for examples (.visit_Call(), visit_Attribute()).  The last one is
    for when one explanation needs to span multiple lines, e.g. when
    displaying diffs.

functions.name: py._io.saferepr.saferepr
documentation:
return a size-limited safe repr-string for the given object.
Failing __repr__ functions of user instances will be represented
with a short exception info and 'saferepr' generally takes
care to never raise exceptions itself.  This function is a wrapper
around the Repr/reprlib functionality of the standard 2.6 lib.

classes.name: py._io.saferepr.SafeRepr
documentation:
subclass of repr.Repr that limits the resulting size of repr()
and includes information on exceptions raised during the call.

functions.name: py._process.cmdexec.cmdexec
documentation:
return unicode output of executing 'cmd' in a separate process.

raise cmdexec.Error exeception if the command failed.
the exception will provide an 'err' attribute containing
the error-output from the command.
if the subprocess module does not provide a proper encoding/unicode strings
sys.getdefaultencoding() will be used, if that does not exist, 'UTF-8'.

modules.name: py._path.cacheutil
documentation:
This module contains multithread-safe cache implementations.

All Caches have

    getorbuild(key, builder)
    delentry(key)

methods and allow configuration when instantiating the cache class.

methods.name: py._path.cacheutil.BasicCache._prunelowestweight
documentation: prune out entries with lowest weight.

classes.name: py._path.cacheutil.BuildcostAccessCache

methods.name: py._path.cacheutil.BuildcostAccessCache._build
documentation: time function to use for measuring build-times

A BuildTime/Access-counting cache implementation.
the weight of a value is computed as the product of

    num-accesses-of-a-value * time-to-build-the-value

The values with the least such weights are evicted
if the cache maxentries threshold is superceded.
For implementation flexibility more than one object
might be evicted at a time.

classes.name: py._path.cacheutil.AgingCache
documentation: This cache prunes out cache entries that are too old.

This class allows to capture writes to sys.stdout|stderr "in-memory"

modifies sys.stdout|stderr|stdin attributes and does not
touch underlying File Descriptors (use StdCaptureFD for that).

Temporary stub class.  Ideally when stdin is accessed, the
capturing should be turned off, with possibly all data captured
so far sent to the screen.  This should be configurable, though,
because in automated test runs it is better to crash than
hang indefinitely.

functions.name: py._code._assertionold.f

classes.name: py._code._assertionold.View
documentation:
View base class.

If C is a subclass of View, then C(x) creates a proxy object around
the object x.  The actual class of the proxy is not C in general,
but a *subclass* of C determined by the rules below.  To avoid confusion
we call view class the class of the proxy (a subclass of C, so of View)
and object class the class of x.

Attributes and methods not found in the proxy are automatically read on x.
Other operations like setting attributes are performed on the proxy, as
determined by its view class.  The object x is available from the proxy
as its __obj__ attribute.

The view class selection is determined by the __view__ tuples and the
optional __viewkey__ method.  By default, the selected view class is the
most specific subclass of C whose __view__ mentions the class of x.
If no such subclass is found, the search proceeds with the parent
object classes.  For example, C(True) will first look for a subclass
of C with __view__ = (..., bool, ...) and only if it doesn't find any
look for one with __view__ = (..., int, ...), and then ..., object,...
If everything fails the class C itself is considered to be the default.

Alternatively, the view class selection can be driven by another aspect
of the object x, instead of the class of x, by overriding __viewkey__.
See last example at the end of this module.

methods.name: py._path.common.PathBase.check
documentation:
check a path for existence and properties.

Without arguments, return True if the path exists, otherwise False.

valid checkers:

```
file=1    # is a file
file=0    # is not a file (may not even exist)
dir=1     # is a dir
link=1    # is a link
exists=1  # exists
```

You can specify multiple checker definitions, for example:

```
path.check(file=1, link=1)  # a link pointing to a file
```

methods.name: py._path.common.PathBase.fnmatch
documentation:
return true if the basename/fullname matches the glob-'pattern'.

valid pattern characters:

```
*       matches everything
?       matches any single character
[seq]   matches any character in seq
[!seq]  matches any char not in seq
```

If the pattern contains a path-separator then the full path
is used for pattern matching and a '\*' is prepended to the
pattern.

if the pattern doesn't contain a path-separator the pattern
is only matched against the basename.

methods.name: py._path.common.PathBase.visit
documentation:
yields all paths below the current one

fil is a filter (glob pattern or callable), if not matching the
path will not be yielded, defaulting to None (everything is
returned)

rec is a filter (glob pattern or callable) that controls whether
a node is descended, defaulting to None

ignore is an Exception class that is ignoredwhen calling dirlist()
on any of the paths (by default, all exceptions are reported)

bf if True will cause a breadthfirst search instead of the
default depthfirst. Default: False

sort if True will sort entries within each directory level.

classes.name: py._code.code.Traceback

methods.name: py._code.code.Traceback.__init__
documentation: initialize from given python traceback object.

methods.name: py._code.code.Traceback.cut
documentation:
return a Traceback instance wrapping part of this Traceback

by provding any combination of path, lineno and firstlineno, the
first frame to start the to-be-returned traceback is determined

this allows cutting the first part of a Traceback instance e.g.
for formatting reasons (removing some uninteresting bits that deal
with handling of the exception/traceback)

methods.name: py._code.code.Traceback.filter
documentation:
return a Traceback instance with certain items removed

fn is a function that gets a single argument, a TracebackItem
instance, and should return True when the item should be added
to the Traceback, False when not

by default this removes all the TracebackItems which are hidden
(see ishidden() above)

methods.name: py._code.code.Traceback.getcrashentry
documentation:
return last non-hidden traceback entry that lead
to the exception of a traceback.

methods.name: py._code.code.Traceback.recursionindex

originates if appropriate, None if no recursion occurred

Traceback objects encapsulate and offer higher level
access to Traceback entries.

methods.name: py._code.code.ExceptionInfo.getrepr

return str()able representation of this exception info.
showlocals: show locals per traceback entry
style: long|short|no|native traceback style
tbfilter: hide entries (where __tracebackhide__ is true)

in case of style==native, tbfilter and showlocals is ignored.

wraps sys.exc_info() objects and offers
help for navigating the traceback.

classes.name: py._path.local.LocalPath

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.__init__
documentation:
Initialize and return a local Path instance.

Path can be relative to the current directory.
If path is None it defaults to the current working directory.
If expanduser is True, tilde-expansion is performed.
Note that Path instances always carry an absolute path.
Note also that passing in a local path object will simply return
the exact same path object. Use new() to get a new copy.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.samefile
documentation: return True if 'other' references the same file as 'self'.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.remove
documentation:
remove a file or directory (or a directory tree if rec=1).
if ignore_errors is True, errors while removing directories will
be ignored.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.computehash
documentation: return hexdigest of hashvalue for this file.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.new
documentation:
create a modified version of this path.
the following keyword arguments modify various path parts:

```
a:/some/path/to/a/file.ext
xx                           drive
xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx            dirname
                  xxxxxxxx   basename
                  xxxx       purebasename
                       xxx   ext
```

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath._getbyspec
documentation: see new for what 'spec' can be.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.dirpath
documentation: return the directory path joined with any given path arguments.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.join
documentation:
return a new path by appending all 'args' as path
components.  if abs=1 is used restart from root if any
of the args is an absolute path.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.open
documentation:
return an opened file with the given mode.

If ensure is True, create parent directories if needed.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.listdir

list directory contents, possibly filter by the given fil func
and possibly sorted.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.size
documentation: return size of the underlying file object

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.mtime
documentation: return last modification time of the path.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.copy
documentation:
copy path to target.

If mode is True, will copy copy permission from path to target.
If stat is True, copy permission, last modification
time, last access time, and flags from path to target.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.rename

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.dump
documentation: pickle object into path location

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.mkdir
documentation: create & return the directory joined with args.

documentation:
write binary data into path.   If ensure is True create

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.write_text
documentation:
write text data into path using the specified encoding.
If ensure is True create missing parent directories.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.write
documentation:
write data into path.   If ensure is True create
missing parent directories.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.ensure
documentation:
ensure that an args-joined path exists (by default as
a file). if you specify a keyword argument 'dir=True'
then the path is forced to be a directory path.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.stat
documentation: Return an os.stat() tuple.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.lstat
documentation: Return an os.lstat() tuple.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.setmtime
documentation:
set modification time for the given path.  if 'mtime' is None
(the default) then the file's mtime is set to current time.

Note that the resolution for 'mtime' is platform dependent.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.chdir
documentation: change directory to self and return old current directory

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.as_cwd
documentation:
Return a context manager, which changes to the path's dir during the
managed "with" context.
On \_\_enter\_\_ it returns the old dir, which might be `None`.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.realpath
documentation: return a new path which contains no symbolic links.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.atime
documentation: return last access time of the path.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.__str__
documentation: return string representation of the Path.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.chmod
documentation:
change permissions to the given mode. If mode is an
integer it directly encodes the os-specific modes.
if rec is True perform recursively.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.pypkgpath
documentation:
return the Python package path by looking for the last
directory upwards which still contains an __init__.py.
Return None if a pkgpath can not be determined.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.pyimport
documentation:
return path as an imported python module.

If modname is None, look for the containing package
and construct an according module name.
The module will be put/looked up in sys.modules.
if ensuresyspath is True then the root dir for importing
the file (taking __init__.py files into account) will
be prepended to sys.path if it isn't there already.
If ensuresyspath=="append" the root dir will be appended
if it isn't already contained in sys.path.
if ensuresyspath is False no modification of syspath happens.

Special value of ensuresyspath=="importlib" is intended
purely for using in pytest, it is capable only of importing
separate .py files outside packages, e.g. for test suite
without any __init__.py file. It effectively allows having
same-named test modules in different places and offers
mild opt-in via this option. Note that it works only in
recent versions of python.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.sysexec
py._path.local.LocalPath.sysfind
documentation:
return a path object found by looking at the systems
underlying PATH specification. If the checker is not None
it will be invoked to filter matching paths.  If a binary
cannot be found, None is returned
Note: This is probably not working on plain win32 systems
but may work on cygwin.

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.get_temproot
documentation:
return the system's temporary directory
(where tempfiles are usually created in)
special class constructors for local filesystem paths

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.mkdtemp
documentation:
return a Path object pointing to a fresh new temporary directory
(which we created ourself).

methods.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.make_numbered_dir
documentation:
return unique directory with a number greater than the current
maximum one.  The number is assumed to start directly after prefix.
if keep is true directories with a number less than (maxnum-keep)
will be removed. If .lock files are used (lock_timeout non-zero),
algorithm is multi-process safe.

inner_classes.name: py._path.local.LocalPath.ImportMismatchError
documentation: raised on pyimport() if there is a mismatch of __file__'s

object oriented interface to os.path and other local filesystem
related information.

```
http://host.com/repo/path/file.ext
|-----------------------|          dirname

```

you may specify the following parts:

http://host.com/repo/path/file.ext
|-----------------------|          dirname
                          |------| basename
                          |--|     purebasename
                              |--| ext

inner_classes.name: py._path.svnwc.SvnPathBase.Checkers
documentation: def _childmaxrev(self):
""" return maximum revision number of childs (or self.rev if no childs) """
rev = self.rev
for name, info in self._listdir_nameinfo():
rev = max(rev, info.created_rev)
return rev
def _getlatestrevision(self):
""" return latest repo-revision for this path. """
url = self.strpath
path = self.__class__(url, None)
# we need a long walk to find the root-repo and revision
while 1:
try:
rev = max(rev, path._childmaxrev())
previous = path
path = path.dirpath()
except (IOError, process.cmdexec.Error):
break
if rev is None:
raise IOError, "could not determine newest repo revision for %s" % self
return rev
Base implementation for SvnPath implementations.
